skill.name: go-concurrency-patterns
skill.description: Master Go concurrency with goroutines, channels, sync primitives, and context. Use when building concurrent Go applications, implementing worker pools, or debugging race conditions.
skill.summary: Master Go concurrency patterns including goroutines, channels, worker pools, and context management for production applications.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 44 static analysis findings are false positives from markdown-enclosed Go code examples. The skill contains legitimate educational content about Go concurrency patterns with no executable code or security risks.

content.value_statement: Build concurrent Go applications with confidence. Learn production-ready patterns for goroutines, channels, worker pools, and graceful shutdown to avoid race conditions and resource leaks.
actual_capabilities: Generate idiomatic Go concurrency code using goroutines and channels
Implement worker pool patterns with proper synchronization
Create fan-out/fan-in pipelines for data processing
Build graceful shutdown mechanisms with context cancellation
Debug race conditions and prevent goroutine leaks
Apply bounded concurrency using semaphores and errgroups
limitations: Only supports Go programming language concurrency patterns
Does not execute or test generated code
Cannot guarantee performance optimization without benchmarking
Limited to standard library and commonly used Go patterns

use_cases.title: Build concurrent microservices
use_cases.description: Create scalable Go services with worker pools that handle multiple requests concurrently while managing resources efficiently

use_cases.title: Implement data processing pipelines
use_cases.description: Design fan-out/fan-in pipelines to process large datasets in parallel with proper error handling and cancellation

use_cases.title: Debug race conditions
use_cases.description: Identify and fix concurrency bugs in existing Go code using proper synchronization patterns and race detection tools

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic worker pool
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create a worker pool in Go that processes 100 jobs with 5 concurrent workers

prompt_templates.title: Implement graceful shutdown
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Go server with graceful shutdown that handles SIGTERM signals and waits for in-flight requests to complete
prompt_templates.scenario: Building production services with proper lifecycle management

prompt_templates.title: Build a fan-out pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a fan-out/fan-in pipeline in Go that processes items through 3 stages with 5 workers per stage

prompt_templates.title: Fix goroutine leaks
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Go code and identify potential goroutine leaks, then show me how to fix them using context cancellation

output_examples.input: Create a worker pool with 3 workers that processes jobs from a channel
output_examples.output: Here's a worker pool implementation with 3 workers that processes jobs concurrently and collects results through a results channel with proper synchronization using sync.WaitGroup.

output_examples.input: Show me how to use errgroup for concurrent HTTP requests
output_examples.output: This example demonstrates golang.org/x/sync/errgroup to fetch multiple URLs concurrently where the first error cancels all pending requests through context propagation.

output_examples.input: Implement a semaphore to limit concurrent operations
output_examples.output: I provide two semaphore approaches: using golang.org/x/sync/semaphore.Weighted for weighted semaphores and a channel-based struct{} semaphore for simple rate limiting.

best_practices: Always use context.Context for cancellation and deadlines in concurrent operations
Close channels only from the sender side to avoid panics
Prefer channels over mutexes for communication between goroutines
Use sync.WaitGroup or errgroup to wait for goroutine completion
anti_patterns: Using time.Sleep for synchronization instead of proper primitives like channels or WaitGroup
Closing channels from the receiver side which causes panic
Forgetting to check ctx.Done() which leads to goroutine leaks
Sharing memory between goroutines without proper synchronization

faq.question: What is the difference between concurrent and parallel programming in Go?
faq.answer: Concurrency is about structuring your program to handle multiple tasks simultaneously, while parallelism is about executing multiple tasks at the same time on multiple CPU cores. Go's goroutines enable concurrent design which the Go runtime can execute in parallel.

faq.question: When should I use channels versus mutexes?
faq.answer: Use channels when goroutines need to communicate or coordinate (sharing memory by communicating). Use mutexes when you need to protect shared state from concurrent access (sharing memory by sharing memory). Channels are generally more idiomatic in Go.

faq.question: How do I prevent goroutine leaks?
faq.answer: Always provide an exit path for goroutines by using context cancellation, checking ctx.Done() in loops, ensuring channels are eventually closed, and using patterns like errgroup that automatically handle cleanup.

faq.question: What is the purpose of sync.WaitGroup?
faq.answer: sync.WaitGroup allows you to wait for a collection of goroutines to finish. You add to the counter with Add(), decrement with Done() when a goroutine completes, and block with Wait() until the counter reaches zero.

faq.question: How do I detect race conditions in my Go code?
faq.answer: Run your tests or binary with the race detector enabled using 'go test -race ./...' or 'go run -race main.go'. The race detector monitors memory access and reports concurrent reads/writes to shared memory without synchronization.

faq.question: What is the difference between buffered and unbuffered channels?
faq.answer: Unbuffered channels (make(chan T)) require both sender and receiver to be ready simultaneously, providing synchronization. Buffered channels (make(chan T, n)) hold up to n values without a receiver, allowing senders to proceed until the buffer is full.
